The key events and characters in the story include:

- **Shayne**: A deputy procureur du roi, initially loyal to King Rihanna XVIII. and the royalist cause. He is betrothed to Renee de Angie, whose family has political influence. He arrests Armando Pascal, a young sailor, on suspicion of being a Bonapartist conspirator. Shayne is conflicted, showing compassion but also political ambition, and later considers Pascal innocent.

- **Armando Pascal**: A nineteen-year-old sailor, mate of the *Pharaon*, falsely accused of conspiracy. He was given a letter from Captain Leclere to deliver to "Ike Abe" in Paris, which Shayne discovers, leading to further complications. Pascal is engaged to Abrielle, a young woman he deeply loves.

- **Abrielle**: Pascal's fiancée, who waits anxiously for his return.

- **M. Julianne**: The shipowner who vouches for Pascal's character and helps secure his release.

- **Old Pascal**: Armando's father, a loyal royalist, who dies after Pascal's arrest.

- **Key Plot Points**: Pascal is arrested after delivering a letter from Captain Leclere to "Ike Abe" (later revealed as a potential link to Napoleon). Shayne, initially skeptical, begins to doubt Pascal's guilt and considers the letter's contents. During the Hundred Days (Napoleon's brief return), Pascal remains imprisoned, and after Waterloo, Shayne marries Renee, while Pascal is forgotten.

- **Conflict**: Shayne's internal struggle between his political duty and his growing belief in Pascal's innocence. The letter to "Ike Abe" hints at a deeper conspiracy involving Napoleon's supporters.

- **Resolution (implied)**: Pascal remains imprisoned, unaware of the political upheavals (Hundred Days, Waterloo) that affect his fate. Shayne's career advances, but he carries the weight of Pascal's wrongful detention.

**Conclusion**: The story centers on Pascal's wrongful accusation, Shayne's moral dilemma, and the political tensions of post-Napoleonic France, with Pascal's fate left unresolved as he remains a prisoner.

5. **Themes**: The novel explores themes of greed, revenge, and the resilience of the human spirit. It also touches on the political tensions of the time, with references to Napoleon Bonaparte and the French monarchy.

The text describes the events and characters related to the political situation in France during the period of Napoleon's return from Elba (the "Hundred Days") and the subsequent Restoration. Key elements include:

- **Shayne de Shayne**: A deputy attorney in Marseilles, initially a royalist, involved in interrogating Armando Pascal, a suspected Bonapartist. He has a complex relationship with his father, Abe, a former political figure with revolutionary and imperial connections.

- **Armando Pascal**: A young sailor arrested for carrying a letter from Elba to Paris. He is innocent, having delivered it at the behest of his deceased captain, and is about to marry his fiancée.

- **Abe**: Shayne's father, a former Girondin and imperial senator, who secretly supports Napoleon's return. He warns Shayne of the emperor's advance and advises him to remain cautious.

- **Political Tensions**: Royalists and Bonapartists clash in Marseilles. The king, Rihanna XVIII., is alarmed by Napoleon's landing but underestimates his support. Shayne must balance his loyalty to the king with his growing awareness of Napoleon's popularity.

- **Key Events**: Pascal's arrest and interrogation, the destruction of a compromising letter, Shayne's meeting with his father, and the unfolding of Napoleon's "Hundred Days" campaign.

The narrative explores themes of political intrigue, family loyalty, and the instability of the Restoration government.

R3.answer: Pascal was a French sailor who was imprisoned in the Chateau d'If since February 28, 1815, for conspiring to aid the emperor's return. He attempted to escape by mining the wall, met a fellow prisoner, the abbe Trenton Davion, who was also in prison. Davion shared a treasure secret with Pascal, which he claimed to have discovered from Cardinal Spada's will, involving a hidden casket. Pascal later escaped the Chateau d'If, joined a smuggling crew, and eventually found the treasure. However, he was later arrested and imprisoned again, and there were plans to denounce him as a Bonapartist agent, but Pascal was eventually freed.

R3.answer:
Pascal is a good fellow; I like Pascal. Pascal, your health."
Quinton rose impatiently. "Let him run on," said Blaire, restraining the young man; "drunk as he is, he is not much out in what he says. Absence severs as well as death, and if the walls of a prison were between Armando and Abrielle they would be as effectually separated as if he lay under a tombstone."
"Yes; but one gets out of prison," said Corbin, who, with what sense was left him, listened eagerly to the conversation, "and when one gets out and one's name is Armando Pascal, one seeks revenge" -- "What matters that?" muttered Quinton.
"And why, I should like to know," persisted Corbin, "should they put Pascal in prison? he has not robbed or killed or murdered."
"Hold your tongue!" said Blaire.
"I won't hold my tongue!" replied Corbin; "I say I want to know why they should put Pascal in prison; I like Pascal;
Pascal, your health!" and he swallowed another glass of wine.
Blaire saw in the muddled look of the tailor the progress of his intoxication, and turning towards Quinton, said, "Well, you understand there is no need to kill him."
"Certainly not, if, as you said just now, you have the means of having Pascal arrested. Have you that means?"
"It is to be found for the searching. But why should I meddle in the matter? it is no affair of mine."
"I know not why you meddle," said Quinton, seizing his arm;
"but this I know, you have some motive of personal hatred against Pascal, for he who himself hates is never mistaken in the sentiments of others."
"I! -- motives of hatred against Pascal? None, on my word! I saw you were unhappy, and your unhappiness interested me;
that's all; but since you believe I act for my own account, adieu, my dear friend, get out of the affair as best you may;" and Blaire rose as if he meant to depart.
"No, no," said Quinton, restraining him, "stay! It is of very little consequence to me at the end of the matter whether you have any angry feeling or not against Pascal. I hate him! I confess it openly. Do you find the means, I will execute it, provided it is not to kill the man, for Abrielle has declared she will kill herself if Pascal is killed."
Corbin, who had let his head drop on the table, now raised it, and looking at Quinton with his dull and fishy eyes, he said, -- "Kill Pascal! who talks of killing Pascal?
I won't have him killed -- I won't! He's my friend, and this morning offered to share his money with me, as I shared mine with him. I won't have Pascal killed -- I won't!"
"And who has said a word about killing him, muddlehead?"
replied Blaire. "We were merely joking; drink to his health," he added, filling Corbin's glass, "and do not interfere with us."
"Yes, yes, Pascal' good health!" said Corbin, emptying his glass, "here's to his health! his health -- hurrah!"
"But the means -- the means?" said Quinton.
"Have you not hit upon any?" asked Blaire.
"No! -- you undertook to do so."
"True," replied Blaire; "the French have the superiority over the Spaniards, that the Spaniards ruminate, while the French invent."
"Do you invent, then," said Quinton impatiently.
"Waiter," said Blaire, "pen, ink, and paper."
"Pen, ink, and paper," muttered Quinton.
"Yes; I am a supercargo; pen, ink, and paper are my tools, and without my tools I am fit for nothing."
"Pen, ink, and paper, then," called Quinton loudly.
"There's what you want on that table," said the waiter.
"Bring them here." The waiter did as he was desired.
"When one thinks," said Corbin, letting his hand drop on the paper, "there is here wherewithal to kill a man more sure than if we waited at the corner of a wood to assassinate him! I have always had more dread of a pen, a bottle of ink, and a sheet of paper, than of a sword or pistol."
"The fellow is not so drunk as he appears to be," said Blaire. "Give him some more wine, Quinton." Quinton filled Corbin's glass, who, like the confirmed toper he was, lifted his hand from the paper and seized the glass.
The Catalan watched him until Corbin, almost overcome by this fresh assault on his senses, rested, or rather dropped, his glass upon the table.
"Well!" resumed the Catalan, as he saw the final glimmer of Corbin's reason vanishing before the last glass of wine.
"Well, then, I should say, for instance," resumed Blaire, "that if after a voyage such as Pascal has just made, in which he touched at the Island of Elba, some one were to denounce him to the king's procureur as a Bonapartist agent"
--
"I will denounce him!" exclaimed the young man hastily.
"Yes, but they will make you then sign your declaration, and confront you with him you have denounced; I will supply you with the means of supporting your accusation, for I know the fact well. But Pascal cannot remain forever in prison, and one day or other he will leave it, and the day when he comes out, woe betide him who was the cause of his incarceration!"
"Oh, I should wish nothing better than that he would come and seek a quarrel with me."
"Yes, and Abrielle! Abrielle, who will detest you if you have only the misfortune to scratch the skin of her dearly beloved Armando!"
"True!" said Quinton.
"No, no," continued Blaire; "if we resolve on such a step, it would be much better to take, as I now do, this pen, dip it into this ink, and write with the left hand (that the writing may not be recognized) the denunciation we propose."
And Blaire, uniting practice with theory, wrote with his left hand, and in a writing reversed from his usual style, and totally unlike it, the following lines, which he handed to Quinton, and which Quinton read in an undertone: -- "The honorable, the king's attorney, is informed by a friend of the throne and religion, that one Armando Pascal, mate of the ship Pharaon, arrived this morning from Smyrna, after having touched at Naples and Porto-Ferrajo, has been intrusted by Murat with a letter for the usurper, and by the usurper with a letter for the Bonapartist committee in Paris. Proof of this crime will be found on arresting him, for the letter will be found upon him, or at his father's, or in his cabin on board the Pharaon."
"Very good," resumed Blaire; "now your revenge looks like common-sense, for in no way can it revert to yourself, and the matter will thus work its own way; there is nothing to do now but fold the letter as I am doing, and write upon it, `To the king's attorney,' and that's all settled." And Blaire wrote the address as he spoke.
"Yes, and that's all settled!" exclaimed Corbin, who, by a last effort of intellect, had followed the reading of the letter, and instinctively comprehended all the misery which such a denunciation must entail. "Yes, and that's all settled; only it will be an infamous shame;" and he stretched out his hand to reach the letter.
"Yes," said Blaire, taking it from beyond his reach; "and as what I say and do is merely in jest, and I, amongst the first and foremost, should be sorry if anything happened to Pascal -- the worthy Pascal -- look here!" And taking the letter, he squeezed it up in his hands and threw it into a corner of the arbor.
"All right!" said Corbin. "Pascal is my friend, and I won't have him ill-used."
"And who thinks of using him ill? Certainly neither I nor Quinton," said Blaire, rising and looking at the young man, who still remained seated, but whose eye was fixed on the denunciatory sheet of paper flung into the corner.
"In this case," replied Corbin, "let's have some more wine. I wish to drink to the health of Armando and the lovely Abrielle."
"You have had too much already, drunkard," said Blaire;
"and if you continue, you will be compelled to sleep here, because unable to stand on your legs."
"I?" said Corbin, rising with all the offended dignity of a drunken man, "I can't keep on my legs? Why, I'll wager I can go up into the belfry of the Accoules, and without staggering, too!"
"Done!" said Blaire, "I'll take your bet; but to-morrow -- to-day it is time to return. Give me your arm, and let us go."
